# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following question bank is modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. Marks are awarded strictly on **concepts, sequence, and terminology**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a restriction endonuclease. With a neat diagram, explain the mechanism of action of *EcoRI* on its recognition sequence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **restriction endonuclease** is a bacterial enzyme that recognizes and cleaves DNA at specific short palindromic sequences. *EcoRI* (from *E. coli* RY13 strain) recognizes the 6-bp palindrome **5'-GAATTC-3'** and cleaves between **G** and **A** on both strands, generating **5' sticky ends** with 4-nucleotide overhangs.

The cut can be represented as:

$$5'\text{-G} \quad \downarrow \text{AATTC-}3'$$
$$3'\text{-CTTAA} \quad \uparrow \text{G-}5'$$

The resulting sticky ends have the sequence **5'-AATT-3'** as a 5' overhang, allowing base-pairing with any other DNA fragment cut by the same enzyme. *EcoRI* requires **Mg²⁺** as a cofactor. (Award 3 marks for definition + correct sequence + diagram.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a cloning vector? List any four essential features of a good plasmid vector.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **cloning vector** is a self-replicating DNA molecule used as a vehicle to carry the foreign DNA (insert) into a host cell for replication and expression.

**Four essential features of a good plasmid vector:**

1. **Origin of Replication (ori):** Enables autonomous replication of the plasmid inside the host cell (e.g., *E. coli*).
2. **Selectable Marker Gene:** Typically an antibiotic resistance gene (*ampR*, *kanR*, *tetR*) to allow identification of transformed cells.
3. **Multiple Cloning Site (MCS / Polylinker):** A short stretch containing multiple unique restriction enzyme sites for easy insertion of the foreign DNA.
4. **Reporter / Marker Gene:** Such as the *lacZ'* gene for blue-white screening, allowing visual differentiation of recombinant (white) vs. non-recombinant (blue) colonies.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Students must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B**. Each contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) Describe in detail the seven major steps involved in the construction of a recombinant DNA molecule. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The construction of recombinant DNA is performed in a sequential, stepwise manner:

**Step 1 — Isolation of the desired DNA (gene of interest):**
Genomic DNA is extracted from the source organism by cell lysis using **detergents (SDS)** and **proteinase K**, followed by **phenol-chloroform extraction** and **ethanol precipitation**. Purity is assessed by the **A260/A280 absorbance ratio (~1.8 for pure DNA)**.

**Step 2 — Cutting the DNA at specific sites using restriction endonucleases:**
The isolated DNA and the plasmid vector are both cut with the **same restriction enzyme** (e.g., *EcoRI*) to generate **compatible sticky ends**. Incubation is typically at **37 °C for 1–2 hours** in a buffer containing **Mg²⁺**.

**Step 3 — Joining the insert into the vector (Ligation):**
The cut insert and vector are mixed in a **3:1 insert:vector molar ratio** with **T4 DNA ligase and ATP**, then incubated at **16 °C overnight** to form a covalent recombinant plasmid.

**Step 4 — Introduction into a host cell (Transformation):**
The recombinant plasmid is introduced into competent *E. coli* cells via **heat shock (42 °C, 45 sec)** in CaCl₂-treated cells, or via **electroporation**.

**Step 5 — Selection of transformants:**
Cells are grown on **LB agar plates containing a selective antibiotic** (e.g., ampicillin). Only transformed cells carrying the plasmid-borne resistance gene will form colonies.

**Step 6 — Screening for recombinants (Blue-White Screening):**
Plates contain **X-gal and IPTG**. Colonies with intact *lacZ'* (no insert) turn **blue**, while colonies with disrupted *lacZ'* (recombinant) appear **white**.

**Step 7 — Verification and Expression:**
White colonies are picked, and the insert is confirmed by **restriction digestion + gel electrophoresis**, **PCR**, or **Sanger sequencing**. If an expression vector is used, the recombinant protein (e.g., human insulin) is produced. **(7 marks: 1 mark per step explained properly.)**

---

**(b) Explain the principle and procedure of Blue-White Screening for the identification of recombinant clones. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
The plasmid vector (e.g., **pUC19**) contains a short segment of the **β-galactosidase gene** (*lacZ'*) that encodes the **α-fragment** of the enzyme. The **Multiple Cloning Site (MCS)** is embedded *within* this *lacZ'* gene. When foreign DNA is inserted into the MCS, it **disrupts** the *lacZ'* reading frame, preventing the production of a functional α-fragment. The α-fragment is complemented by the ω-fragment encoded by the *E. coli* host chromosome (e.g., DH5α strain), restoring β-galactosidase activity only when *lacZ'* is intact. This is called **α-complementation**.

**Procedure:**

1. **Plate transformation mixture** on LB agar containing:
   - The **selective antibiotic** (e.g., ampicillin, 100 μg/mL).
   - **X-gal** (5-bromo-4-chloro-3-indolyl-β-D-galactopyranoside), a chromogenic substrate at **40 μg/mL**.
   - **IPTG** (Isopropyl β-D-1-thiogalactopyranoside), a non-hydrolyzable inducer of the *lac* operon at **0.5 mM**.

2. **Incubate** plates at **37 °C for 16–18 hours**.

3. **Observe colony color:**
   - **Blue colonies** = vector self-ligated, *lacZ'* intact → β-galactosidase cleaves X-gal into **5,5'-dibromo-4,4'-dichloro-indigo** (blue pigment) + galactose.
   - **White colonies** = recombinant plasmid (insert present), *lacZ'* disrupted → no β-galactosidase → no cleavage of X-gal → colony remains white.

4. **Pick white colonies** for further verification by **mini-prep DNA extraction, restriction digestion, PCR, and sequencing.**

**Why this is powerful:** It is a **single-step visual screen** — no biochemical assay or radioactive probe required. **(7 marks: 2 marks for principle, 3 marks for procedure, 1 mark for chemistry, 1 mark for verification.)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Discuss in detail the different types of cloning vectors used in recombinant DNA technology, comparing their host systems and insert capacity. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Cloning vectors are classified based on the **host organism** they propagate in and the **size of the insert** they can accommodate:

**1. Plasmid Vectors (Host: *E. coli*)**
- **Examples:** pBR322, pUC18, pUC19, pBluescript.
- **Insert capacity:** Up to **~10 kb**.
- **Features:** Circular, double-stranded DNA; carry an *ori*, selectable marker (antibiotic resistance), MCS, and reporter (*lacZ'*).
- **Use:** Routine cloning, subcloning, and small gene inserts.

**2. Lambda (λ) Phage Vectors (Host: *E. coli*)**
- **Examples:** λEMBL, λZAP, λgt10/11.
- **Insert capacity:** **~9–23 kb** (replacing the central non-essential region of the λ genome).
- **Features:** Linear dsDNA (~48.5 kb); can be propagated lytically or lysogenically; efficient infection of *E. coli* gives higher transformation efficiency than plasmids.
- **Use:** Construction of **genomic and cDNA libraries**.

**3. Cosmid Vectors (Host: *E. coli*)**
- **Insert capacity:** **~30–45 kb**.
- **Features:** Plasmid + λ phage *cos* (cohesive end) sites; packaged into phage heads but replicate as plasmids.
- **Use:** Cloning of large eukaryotic genes.

**4. BAC — Bacterial Artificial Chromosome (Host: *E. coli*)**
- **Insert capacity:** **~100–300 kb** (up to **~700 kb** in some systems).
- **Features:** Based on the naturally large, low-copy *E. coli* F-plasmid; extremely stable inserts with minimal rearrangements.
- **Use:** **Human Genome Project**, structural genomics.

**5. YAC — Yeast Artificial Chromosome (Host: *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*)**
- **Insert capacity:** **~200 kb – 2 Mb** (megabase range).
- **Features:** Engineered to contain a yeast **centromere (CEN)**, **telomeres (TEL)**, and an **autonomously replicating sequence (ARS)**, mimicking a mini-chromosome.
- **Use:** Cloning of the **largest DNA fragments**, including complete eukaryotic genes with all regulatory elements.

**6. Expression Vectors (Variable hosts)**
- Contain a **promoter** (e.g., T7, lac, trp), a **RBS**, **start codon (ATG)**, and **transcription terminator**. Some include a **His-tag** or **GST-tag** for protein purification.
- **Use:** Production of recombinant proteins (e.g., **human insulin in *E. coli***, **hepatitis B vaccine in yeast**).

**7. Plant Vectors — Ti Plasmid (Host: Plant cells, via *Agrobacterium tumefaciens*)**
- A segment of the Ti plasmid (**T-DNA**) is transferred into the plant genome.
- Used to create **transgenic plants** such as Bt cotton, Bt brinjal, and **Golden Rice** (engineered for β-carotene production). **(7 marks: 1 mark per vector type with insert size, 0.5 mark extra for example/host.)**

---

**(b) Explain the process of cDNA synthesis from eukaryotic mRNA and discuss why cDNA is preferred over genomic DNA for expressing eukaryotic proteins in bacterial hosts. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Why cDNA is needed:**
Bacterial hosts (e.g., *E. coli*) **cannot perform RNA splicing** — they lack the **spliceosome** machinery to remove introns. If a eukaryotic genomic DNA (containing introns) is cloned and expressed in *E. coli*, the resulting mRNA will still contain introns, leading to **aberrant, non-functional, or truncated proteins**. Therefore, the **introns must be removed at the mRNA stage in the eukaryotic cell**, and the mature mRNA is then reverse-transcribed into **cDNA** (intron-free).

**The cDNA Synthesis Procedure (step-by-step):**

**Step 1 — mRNA Isolation:**
Total RNA is extracted from the eukaryotic cell using **guanidinium thiocyanate-phenol-chloroform (TRIzol)**, and mRNA is enriched via **oligo(dT) cellulose chromatography** (mRNA has a 3' poly-A tail).

**Step 2 — First-Strand Synthesis:**
An **oligo(dT₁₈) primer** anneals to the poly-A tail of the mRNA. **Reverse Transcriptase (RT)** adds dNTPs in the **5'→3' direction**, synthesizing a complementary DNA strand:

$$\text{mRNA} + \text{oligo(dT)} \xrightarrow{\text{RT, dNTPs, 42–50 °C}} \text{RNA–DNA hybrid}$$

**Step 3 — Removal of the mRNA Template:**
The mRNA strand is degraded by **RNase H** (introduces nicks) followed by **DNA Polymerase I** (which uses the nicked RNA as primers) or by **alkaline hydrolysis**, leaving a **single-stranded cDNA**.

**Step 4 — Second-Strand Synthesis:**
**DNA Polymerase I** synthesizes the second DNA strand using the first cDNA as a template, generating **double-stranded cDNA**:

$$\text{ss cDNA} \xrightarrow{\text{DNA Pol I, dNTPs, 16 °C}} \text{ds cDNA}$$

**Step 5 — Modification for Cloning:**
The ds-cDNA is treated with **S1 nuclease** to blunt the ends (optional, depending on vector), and **linkers or adaptors** containing restriction enzyme sites are ligated to the ends. The cDNA is then ligated into a plasmid vector.

**Why cDNA is preferred for bacterial expression (summary):**
- It is **intron-free**, so bacteria can transcribe and translate it into the correct protein.
- It represents only the **mature, spliced mRNA**, encoding the functional protein.
- It allows **tissue-specific** or **developmental stage-specific** expression profiling (cDNA libraries).
- It enables the production of eukaryotic proteins (e.g., insulin, growth hormone) in prokaryotic factories. **(7 marks: 3 marks for stepwise cDNA synthesis, 2 marks for intron rationale, 2 marks for applications.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in rDNA Technology Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the cleavage pattern of *EcoRI* with *HindIII*.** Always remember *EcoRI* leaves **5'-AATT-3' overhangs**, while *SmaI* (a blunt cutter) leaves **blunt ends** at **5'-CCC↓GGG-3'**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Mg²⁺ cofactor** for Type II restriction enzymes — examiners explicitly look for this.
> 3. **Confusing Type I, II, and III restriction enzymes.** Type I cuts *away* from the site; Type II cuts *within*; Type III cuts ~25 bp *downstream*. This is a **favourite 3-mark question**.
> 4. **Skipping the role of IPTG** in blue-white screening. Students often say "X-gal gives blue colour" but forget IPTG is the *inducer* of *lacZ'*.
> 5. **Writing "reverse transcriptase transcribes DNA"** — NO, it **transcribes RNA → cDNA**. This is the most common single-line error.
> 6. **Ignoring that alkaline phosphatase** is used to dephosphorylate the vector to prevent self-ligation. KTU examiners reward this mention.
> 7. **Mixing up YAC and BAC capacities** — YAC = megabase range, BAC = 100–300 kb. Easy to swap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- **rDNA Technology** = combining DNA from 2+ sources *in vitro*; foundational technique of modern biotechnology.
- **Three Nobel Prizes** are tied to rDNA: Arber, Smith, Nathans (1978); Berg, Gilbert, Sanger (1980 — Berg made first rDNA molecule); Mullis (1993 — PCR).
- **Type II restriction enzymes** are the workhorse of cloning; require **Mg²⁺**; recognize **4–8 bp palindromes**.
- **5'–GAATTC–3'** is the *EcoRI* site; cuts between G and A; gives **5'-AATT** sticky ends.
- **T4 DNA ligase** seals nicks using **ATP** as a cofactor; catalyzes **phosphodiester bond** formation.
- **Reverse transcriptase** is needed to convert **mRNA → cDNA**; essential for eukaryotic gene expression in prokaryotes.
- **pBR322** was the first artificial plasmid vector constructed; **pUC19** is the modern standard for blue-white screening.
- **Insert size rule of thumb:** Plasmid < Plasmid + λ phage < Cosmid < BAC < YAC (i.e., **< 10 kb < 23 kb < 45 kb < 300 kb < 2 Mb**).
- **Blue-White Screening:** Insert disrupts *lacZ'* → white colony; no insert → blue colony (X-gal cleaved by β-galactosidase).
- **IPTG** is the inducer; **X-gal** is the chromogenic substrate; **ampicillin** is the selective antibiotic.
- **Heat-shock transformation:** 42 °C, 45 sec, in **CaCl₂-competent** *E. coli*. **Electroporation:** 2.5 kV pulse.
- **A260/A280 ≈ 1.8** = pure DNA; **≈ 2.0** = pure RNA — pure nucleic acid quality check.
- **First commercial product:** Recombinant human insulin (**Humulin**) by Eli Lilly (1982).
- **Human Genome Project (2003)** sequenced ~**3.2 billion bp** using BACs and YACs as primary cloning vectors.
- **DNA Ligase Equation for Phosphodiester Bond:** 3'-OH + 5'-PO₄ → 3'-5' phosphodiester bond + AMP + PPi.
- **Selection ≠ Screening:** Selection (antibiotic) identifies transformed cells; Screening (blue-white, PCR) identifies recombinant plasmids.
- **Agrobacterium Ti plasmid** = natural genetic engineer; T-DNA integrates into plant genome; basis of transgenic plant technology.
- **PstI** cuts within the *ampR* gene of pBR322 — a classic insertional inactivation marker used before blue-white screening existed.

> **Final Tip:** When the question is 14 marks with a sub-part, allocate **1–2 minutes for planning**, then **spend 4–5 minutes per sub-part**, and **reserve 2 minutes for a final review**. KTU examiners reward **diagrams, equations, and underlined keywords**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monoclonal Antibodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT743 - INTRODUCTION TO BIONANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Monoclonal Antibodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Monoclonal Antibody (mAb)** is an antibody produced by a single B-lymphocyte clone that exhibits absolute monospecificity — meaning every immunoglobulin molecule in the population recognizes and binds to **one identical epitope (antigenic determinant)** on a single antigen. These are biologically engineered proteins synthesized *in vitro* using the **Hybridoma Technology** pioneered by **Georges Köhler and César Milstein (1975)**, for which they were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1984 (shared with Niels Jerne).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> *“Monoclonal antibodies are homogeneous populations of immunoglobulin molecules derived from a single parent B-cell clone, possessing identical heavy and light chain variable regions, and therefore a single, well-defined antigen specificity. They are produced by fusing an antibody-producing B-lymphocyte with an immortal myeloma cell to form a hybridoma.”*

### 1.2 Structural Anatomy of a Monoclonal Antibody

A canonical immunoglobulin G (IgG) monoclonal antibody is a **Y-shaped glycoprotein** of molecular weight approximately **150 kDa**, composed of:
- **2 Heavy Chains (H)** — ~50 kDa each (constant + variable region)
- **2 Light Chains (L)** — ~25 kDa each (kappa κ or lambda λ)
- **2 Antigen-Binding Fragments (Fab)** — the "arms" of the Y
- **1 Crystallizable Fragment (Fc)** — the "stem" — mediates effector functions
- Connected by **disulfide bridges** and stabilized by non-covalent interactions

The **Complementarity-Determining Regions (CDRs)** located in the variable domains (VH and VL) form the **paratope** — the 3D lock that fits a single molecular **epitope**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Guided Missile" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Smart Missile:**
> Imagine a battlefield with hundreds of enemy tanks (pathogens/cancer cells), but each tank has a *slightly different* armor signature (epitope).
> - **Polyclonal antibodies (pAbs)** = A *barrage of unguided rockets* — many different rockets, each locked onto a *different part* of the tank. They create collateral damage and noise.
> - **Monoclonal antibodies (mAbs)** = A *single laser-guided cruise missile* — identical rockets, *all* designed to hit *one precise* weak point (single epitope) of the tank with surgical accuracy.
>
> This is why mAbs are the cornerstone of modern **targeted therapy**, **immunodiagnostics**, and **nanomedicine**.

### 1.4 Key Distinguishing Features (Polyclonal vs. Monoclonal)

| Property | Polyclonal Antibody (pAb) | Monoclonal Antibody (mAb) |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Multiple B-cell clones | **Single B-cell clone** (hybridoma) |
| **Epitope Specificity** | Multiple epitopes (poly-specific) | **Single, unique epitope** |
| **Homogeneity** | Heterogeneous mixture | **Homogeneous, identical** |
| **Batch Reproducibility** | Low (varies animal-to-animal) | **High (immortal cell line)** |
| **Affinity** | Mixed affinities (high + low) | **Uniform, high affinity** |
| **Production Cost** | Low | **High** (cell culture facilities) |
| **Cross-reactivity** | High | **Minimal** |
| **KTU Use Case** | Research, basic diagnostics | **Therapeutics, precision diagnostics, nanocarriers** |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** If the KTU question asks *"Why are monoclonal antibodies preferred over polyclonal antibodies in therapeutics?"* — always answer: **(1) Single epitope specificity → fewer off-target side effects**, and **(2) Immortal hybridoma line → unlimited, reproducible supply of identical antibody**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hybridoma Technology — The Production Engine

The **hybridoma technology** is the biological factory that produces mAbs. It is built on **three biological principles**:

1. **B-lymphocytes** (from immunized mouse spleen) → produce *one specific antibody*, but **die in 10–14 days** in culture (finite lifespan).
2. **Myeloma cells** (e.g., SP2/0, NS0, P3X63Ag8) → **immortal** (can proliferate indefinitely) but have been engineered to *lack* the enzyme **Hypoxanthine-Guanine Phosphoribosyltransferase (HGPRT)**.
3. **Polyethylene Glycol (PEG)** → acts as a fusogen, merging the plasma membranes of B-cells and myeloma cells.

**The Magic of Fusion:**
$$\text{B-lymphocyte (antibody}^{+}, \text{finite life}) \;\;+\;\; \text{Myeloma (HGPRT}^{-}, \text{immortal}) \;\;\xrightarrow{\text{PEG}}\;\; \text{Hybridoma}$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Hybridoma Production

**STEP 1 — Immunization (Weeks 0–4):**
- Inject the target antigen (e.g., a cancer biomarker like **HER2**, or a viral protein) into a mouse (typically **BALB/c** strain) along with an **adjuvant** (e.g., Freund's complete/incomplete adjuvant) to boost immune response.
- Repeat **booster doses** every 2 weeks; monitor **antibody titer** via ELISA.
- **Goal:** Maximize activated, antigen-specific B-lymphocytes in the spleen.

**STEP 2 — Cell Harvest & Fusion (Day 0 of fusion):**
- Sacrifice mouse; excise spleen under aseptic conditions.
- Dissociate spleen into a single-cell suspension (mechanical + enzymatic).
- Mix with myeloma cells (typically **1:5 to 1:10 B-cell : myeloma ratio**).
- Add **PEG-1450 (50% w/v)** for **60–90 seconds** — membrane fusion occurs.
- Dilute PEG gradually to stop fusion; transfer to HAT selection medium.

**STEP 3 — HAT Selection (Days 1–14):**
- The **HAT medium** contains:
  - **H**ypoxanthine
  - **A**minopterin (blocks *de novo* DNA synthesis)
  - **T**hymidine
- Cells now must use the **salvage pathway** (which requires HGPRT) to survive.
- **Selection logic table:**

| Cell Type | HGPRT Status | Aminopterin Effect | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unfused Myeloma | HGPRT⁻ | Blocked — cannot use salvage or de novo | **Dies** |
| Unfused B-cell | HGPRT⁺ | Blocked de novo, but salvage works | **Dies** (lifespan) |
| B-B fusion | HGPRT⁺ | Survives briefly, then dies | **Dies** (no immortality) |
| **Hybridoma (B + Myeloma)** | **HGPRT⁺ + Immortal** | **Salvage works + Immortal** | **SURVIVES** ✓ |

**STEP 4 — Screening (Day 14):**
- Test hybridoma supernatants for the desired antibody using **ELISA**, **Western Blot**, or **flow cytometry**.
- Positive wells are identified — these contain the **clones of interest**.

**STEP 5 — Cloning (Single-Cell Cloning):**
- Use **limiting dilution** or **soft agar cloning** to ensure **monoclonality** (one cell per well).
- Re-screen to confirm stability.

**STEP 6 — Scale-up Production:**
- **In vitro:** Grow hybridomas in bioreactors (e.g., hollow-fiber, stirred-tank) → yield **10–100 mg/L**.
- **In vivo (Ascites method):** Inject hybridomas into mouse peritoneal cavity → yield **5–25 mg/mL** (now ethically restricted — KTU 2024 prefers *in vitro*).
- **Modern:** Use **Chinese Hamster Ovary (CHO)** cells with recombinant DNA technology for industrial-scale production.

### 2.3 Types of Monoclonal Antibodies (Naming Convention Suffix: *-mab*)

| Generation | Name Pattern | Composition | % Human | Example Drug |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Murine** | *-omab* | 100% Mouse | 0% | Muromonab-CD3 |
| **Chimeric** | *-ximab* | Mouse Fab + Human Fc | ~65–70% | Rituximab, Infliximab |
| **Humanized** | *-zumab* | Only mouse CDRs grafted onto human framework | ~90–95% | Trastuzumab (Herceptin) |
| **Fully Human** | *-umab* | 100% Human (phage display / transgenic mice) | 100% | Adalimumab (Humira) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Naming Convention Rule:** The suffix tells you the species origin. **"-ximab" = Chimeric, "-zumab" = Humanized, "-umab" = Fully Human.** Examiners love this question!

### 2.4 Monoclonal Antibodies in Bionanotechnology — The Conjugation Paradigm

In **nanobiotechnology**, mAbs serve as **bio-recognition elements** that are conjugated to nanoparticles to create **active targeting systems**.

**Common mAb–Nanoparticle Conjugates:**

- **mAb–Gold Nanoparticle (AuNP):** Used in **lateral flow immunoassays** (e.g., pregnancy tests, COVID-19 rapid antigen tests) and **photothermal therapy**.
- **mAb–Liposome (Immunoliposome):** Targeted drug delivery (e.g., Doxil variants for HER2+ breast cancer).
- **mAb–Quantum Dot (QD):** Multiplexed fluorescence imaging of tumor biomarkers.
- **mAb–Magnetic Nanoparticle (SPION):** MRI contrast agents + magnetic hyperthermia.
- **mAb–Polymeric Nanoparticle (PLGA):** Sustained release targeted chemotherapy.

The conjugation is achieved via:
- **EDC/NHS chemistry** (carboxyl-amine coupling)
- **Maleimide-thiol coupling** (cysteine residues on mAb)
- **Click chemistry (azide-alkyne cycloaddition)** — most specific
- **Streptavidin-biotin linkage** — high affinity ($\sim 10^{15}$ M⁻¹)

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **mAb Molecular Weight** | $\text{MW}_{\text{IgG}} \approx 150,000$ | Daltons (Da) | Dosage calculations |
| **Two binding sites** | $N = 2$ per mAb | dimensionless | Avidity calculation |
| **Affinity (K$_D$)** | $K_D = \dfrac{k_{off}}{k_{on}}$ | M (molar) | Binding strength |
| **Avidity** | $K_{av} \approx (K_a)^N$ for N sites | M⁻ᴺ | Functional strength |
| **Antibody Titer (ELISA)** | $\text{Titer} = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{\text{highest dilution giving positive signal}}\right)$ | log scale | Immune response monitoring |
| **Hybridoma Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{N_{\text{positive wells}}}{N_{\text{total wells}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Fusion efficiency |
| **HAT Selection Principle** | $\text{Survive only if: } \text{HGPRT}^{+} \cap \text{Immortal}$ | Boolean | Cell line purity |
| **mAb–NP Coupling Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{n_{\text{mAb}}}{n_{\text{NP}}}$ | mAb per NP | Targeting density |
| **Tumor Targeting EPR Effect** | Enhanced Permeability & Retention (passive) | — | Nano-drug accumulation |
| **Targeting Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Accumulation}_{\text{target}}}{\text{Accumulation}_{\text{non-target}}}$ | ratio (ideally > 5) | Specificity measure |
| **Photothermal Conversion** | $P = \eta_{abs} \cdot I \cdot A$ | Watts | AuNP-mAb therapy |
| **Size constraint** | NP diameter: 10 – 200 nm (optimal EPR) | nm | Nano-conjugate design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:** The **EPR (Enhanced Permeability and Retention) effect** is the *passive* targeting mechanism that allows nanoparticles to accumulate in tumor tissue due to leaky vasculature and poor lymphatic drainage. mAbs add the *active* targeting layer on top of this.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oncology:** Trastuzumab (Herceptin) targets HER2⁺ breast cancer.
- **Autoimmune:** Adalimumab blocks TNF-α in rheumatoid arthritis.
- **Infectious Disease:** Casirivimab/Imdevimab for SARS-CoV-2.
- **Diagnostics:** mAb-conjugated AuNPs in pregnancy/COVID rapid tests.
- **Imaging:** mAb-Qdots for intraoperative tumor margin detection.
- **Theranostics:** Single agent for *therapy* + *diagnosis* (e.g., mAb-SPION).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: HAT Selection — Mathematical Logic of Cell Survival

**Problem:** Prove that only the B-cell + Myeloma hybridoma survives in HAT medium.

**Given:**
- Myeloma cells lack the enzyme **HGPRT** (Hypoxanthine-Guanine Phosphoribosyltransferase).
- Aminopterin blocks the **de novo** DNA synthesis pathway.
- Cells must use the **salvage pathway** to survive.

**De novo pathway (blocked by Aminopterin):**
$$\text{IMP} \xrightarrow{\text{Aminopterin blocks}} \text{GMP, AMP}$$

**Salvage pathway (requires HGPRT):**
$$\text{Hypoxanthine} + \text{PRPP} \xrightarrow{\text{HGPRT}} \text{IMP} \rightarrow \text{GMP}$$

$$\text{Guanine} + \text{PRPP} \xrightarrow{\text{HGPRT}} \text{GMP}$$

**Survival condition:**
$$\text{Survival} \iff (\text{HGPRT}^{+} \;\cap\; \text{Immortal})$$

**Truth Table Analysis:**

| Cell Type | HGPRT | Immortal | Aminopterin Blocks De Novo | Salvage Pathway | Survival |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unfused Myeloma | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ (no salvage) | ✗ | **Dies** |
| Unfused B-cell | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ (briefly) | **Dies (apoptosis, day ~14)** |
| Myeloma-Myeloma Fusion | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | **Dies** |
| B-B Fusion | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | **Dies (no immortality)** |
| **B-Myeloma (Hybridoma)** | **✓** | **✓** | **✓** | **✓** | **✓ SURVIVES** |

**Conclusion:** Only the B-Myeloma hybridoma satisfies both conditions. Hence, after ~14 days in HAT, every surviving cell in the culture is a hybridoma producing *one* specific antibody.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Antibody Titer Calculation (ELISA-Based)

**Problem:** A mouse is immunized with antigen X. Serum dilutions are tested by ELISA. The highest dilution giving a positive signal (absorbance above cutoff 0.2 at 450 nm) is **1:128,000**. Calculate the antibody titer.

**Step 1 — Convert dilution to decimal:**
$$\text{Dilution factor} = \dfrac{1}{128{,}000} = 7.8125 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the titer formula:**
$$\text{Titer} = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{\text{dilution}}\right) = \log_{10}(128{,}000)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate logarithm:**
$$\text{Titer} = \log_{10}(1.28 \times 10^{5}) = \log_{10}(1.28) + 5$$

$$\text{Titer} = 0.1072 + 5 = 5.1072$$

**Result:** Titer ≈ **5.11** (i.e., $\sim 1.3 \times 10^5$).

**Step 4 — Interpretation for fusion decision:**
- Titer > 4.0 → **Strong immune response → Proceed to splenectomy & fusion**.
- Titer < 2.0 → **Re-immunize the mouse with booster doses**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: mAb–Gold Nanoparticle (AuNP) Coupling Efficiency

**Problem:** A batch of **13 nm AuNPs** ($\varepsilon_{520} = 2.7 \times 10^{8}$ M⁻¹cm⁻¹) is to be conjugated with a HER2-targeting mAb (MW = 150 kDa). The AuNP concentration is 10 nM; the mAb is added at 50 μg/mL. Calculate the coupling ratio $R$.

**Step 1 — Convert mAb concentration to molarity:**
$$[\text{mAb}] = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{g/mL}}{150{,}000\;\text{g/mol}} = 3.33 \times 10^{-10}\;\text{mol/mL} = 3.33 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{M}$$

$$[\text{mAb}] = 333\;\text{nM}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate AuNP concentration in nM:**
$$[\text{AuNP}] = 10\;\text{nM}$$

**Step 3 — Determine molar ratio:**
$$R = \dfrac{[\text{mAb}]}{[\text{AuNP}]} = \dfrac{333\;\text{nM}}{10\;\text{nM}} = 33.3$$

**Step 4 — Adjust for conjugation efficiency (assume η = 60%):**
$$R_{\text{conjugated}} = R \times \eta = 33.3 \times 0.60 = 20\;\text{mAb per AuNP}$$

**Step 5 — Verify with optical density:**
$$\text{OD}_{520} = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l = 2.7 \times 10^{8} \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 1 = 2.7$$

**Result:** Approximately **20 mAb molecules are conjugated per AuNP** — sufficient density for active targeting.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Avidity Calculation for a Bivalent IgG

**Given:** A monoclonal IgG has a monovalent affinity $K_a = 10^{8}$ M⁻¹ for its epitope. Calculate the avidity.

**For bivalent binding ($N = 2$):**
$$K_{av} \approx (K_a)^2 = (10^8)^2 = 10^{16}\;\text{M}^{-2}$$

**Effective functional affinity in M⁻¹ (avidity index):**
$$K_{av}^{eff} = K_a \cdot \sqrt{K_a \cdot c_{\text{epitope}}} \;\;(\text{for high epitope density})$$

For a cell surface with $c_{epitope} \approx 10^{-6}$ M effective local concentration:
$$K_{av}^{eff} = 10^8 \cdot \sqrt{10^8 \times 10^{-6}} = 10^8 \cdot 10^1 = 10^9\;\text{M}^{-1}$$

**Conclusion:** A 10-fold enhancement in functional binding strength — explains why mAbs are excellent for **cell-surface targeting** (e.g., cancer cells expressing HER2 at ~10⁶ copies/cell).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flow for mAb Selection in Nanomedicine

| Design Parameter | Question | Decision Point |
|---|---|---|
| **Target** | Is it soluble protein or cell surface? | Cell surface → Bivalent IgG preferred |
| **Species of patient** | Mouse or human therapy? | Human → Use chimeric/humanized/-umab |
| **Payload** | Drug, imaging agent, or both? | Both → Use **theranostic** construct |
| **Nanoparticle core** | Au, liposome, QD, SPION? | Au → Photothermal; QD → Imaging |
| **Conjugation chemistry** | Random amine vs. site-specific cysteine? | Site-specific → Preserve antigen binding |
| **Administration route** | IV, oral, intratumoral? | IV → mAb must be humanized (avoid ADAR) |
| **Regulatory pathway** | Biosimilar or novel? | mAb is novel → Full clinical trials (10+ years) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] (3 Marks)**
**Define monoclonal antibodies. How are they different from polyclonal antibodies?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Monoclonal antibodies (mAbs)** are homogeneous antibody preparations produced by a single B-lymphocyte clone (hybridoma), in which all immunoglobulin molecules are **identical** and recognize **one specific epitope** on a given antigen. They are produced via **hybridoma technology** (Köhler & Milstein, 1975) by fusing antibody-producing B-cells with immortal myeloma cells.
>
> **Differences (any two for full marks):**
>
> | Feature | Monoclonal | Polyclonal |
> |---|---|---|
> | Source | **Single B-cell clone** | Multiple B-cell clones |
> | Specificity | **One epitope** (monospecific) | Multiple epitopes (polyspecific) |
> | Reproducibility | **High** (immortal line) | Low (animal-dependent) |
> | Cross-reactivity | **Minimal** | High |
> | Production | Hybridoma / CHO cells | Animal serum |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model] (3 Marks)**
**Explain the role of HAT medium in hybridoma selection.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **HAT medium** is a selective growth medium containing **Hypoxanthine, Aminopterin, and Thymidine**, used to isolate hybridoma cells from a fused cell mixture.
>
> - **Aminopterin** blocks the **de novo DNA synthesis pathway**.
> - Cells must therefore use the **salvage pathway**, which requires the enzyme **HGPRT (Hypoxanthine-Guanine Phosphoribosyltransferase)**.
> - **Unfused myeloma cells** lack HGPRT → **die**.
> - **Unfused B-cells** have HGPRT but lack immortality → **die within 10–14 days**.
> - **Fused B-Myeloma hybridomas** are **HGPRT⁺ AND immortal** → **survive**.
>
> Hence HAT ensures that **only hybridomas** proliferate, producing the desired antibody.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a) Describe the production of monoclonal antibodies by hybridoma technology. Add a note on the different types of monoclonal antibodies. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) are produced by **hybridoma technology** (Köhler & Milstein, 1975), which involves the fusion of B-lymphocytes (which produce specific antibodies but have limited lifespan) with myeloma cells (which are immortal but lack HGPRT), resulting in hybridoma cells that are both immortal and antibody-producing.

**Step-by-Step Production (4 Marks):**

1. **Immunization:** A BALB/c mouse is immunized with the target antigen emulsified in Freund's adjuvant. Booster doses are given every 2 weeks. Antibody titer is monitored via ELISA.
2. **Cell Fusion:** The spleen is removed, and B-lymphocytes are harvested. These are mixed with **HGPRT-deficient myeloma cells** (e.g., SP2/0) in a **1:5 to 1:10 ratio**. **PEG (polyethylene glycol, 50% w/v)** is added for 60–90 seconds to induce membrane fusion.
3. **HAT Selection:** The fused cells are cultured in **HAT medium**. Aminopterin blocks the *de novo* pathway; only HGPRT⁺ cells survive via the salvage pathway. Unfused myelomas die (HGPRT⁻); unfused B-cells die (finite lifespan). **Only hybridomas (B + Myeloma) survive.**
4. **Screening:** Supernatants from hybridoma-containing wells are screened by **ELISA, Western blot, or flow cytometry** for the desired antibody specificity.
5. **Cloning:** Positive wells are cloned by **limiting dilution** to ensure monoclonality.
6. **Scale-up:** Hybridomas are expanded *in vitro* in bioreactors (e.g., hollow-fiber systems) or *in vivo* in mouse ascites (now ethically restricted).
7. **Purification:** Antibodies are purified using **Protein A/G affinity chromatography**.

**Types of Monoclonal Antibodies (2 Marks):**

| Type | % Human | Suffix | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Murine** | 0% | -omab | Muromonab-CD3 |
| **Chimeric** | 65–70% | -ximab | Rituximab |
| **Humanized** | 90–95% | -zumab | Trastuzumab |
| **Fully Human** | 100% | -umab | Adalimumab |

**Conclusion (0 Mark — wrap up):**
Modern industrial production uses **recombinant CHO cells** to express the cloned antibody genes, providing a scalable, animal-free manufacturing platform.

---

**(b) Discuss the application of monoclonal antibodies in nanobiotechnology, with at least three examples of mAb-nanoparticle conjugates. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
In nanobiotechnology, monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) serve as **bio-recognition / active targeting elements** that are conjugated to nanoparticles (NPs), enabling the resulting nanoconjugates to selectively bind to specific cell-surface markers (e.g., HER2, EGFR, CD20) overexpressed on diseased cells such as cancer cells.

**Three Examples of mAb–Nanoparticle Conjugates (4.5 Marks — 1.5 each):**

**1. mAb–Gold Nanoparticles (mAb–AuNPs) — Diagnostics + Photothermal Therapy**
- AuNPs (10–50 nm) are conjugated with anti-PSA or anti-HER2 mAbs via **thiol-gold chemistry** or **EDC/NHS coupling**.
- **Application 1 (Diagnostics):** **Lateral flow immunoassays** — the COVID-19 rapid antigen test and pregnancy test use mAb-AuNP conjugates as the colorimetric reporter. Limit of detection: ~pg/mL.
- **Application 2 (Therapy):** Upon **NIR laser (808 nm) irradiation**, AuNPs convert light to heat via **surface plasmon resonance**, killing the targeted tumor cells (photothermal therapy).

**2. mAb–Liposomes (Immunoliposomes) — Targeted Drug Delivery**
- Liposomal carriers loaded with **doxorubicin** or **paclitaxel** are decorated with mAbs (e.g., anti-HER2 trastuzumab) on the surface via **PEG-maleimide linkers**.
- Example: **MM-302** — anti-HER2 immunoliposome for HER2⁺ metastatic breast cancer.
- Advantage: Increases tumor accumulation 5–10× and reduces cardiotoxicity of free doxorubicin.

**3. mAb–Quantum Dots (mAb–QDs) — Multiplexed Tumor Imaging**
- Semiconductor QDs (CdSe/ZnS, 5–10 nm) are conjugated with mAbs against multiple biomarkers.
- Used in **immunofluorescence imaging** of tumor margins during surgery.
- Advantage over organic dyes: Higher quantum yield, photostability, and **multiplexing capability** (different emission colors for different markers).

**Other Examples (0.5 Mark — for bonus):**
- **mAb–SPIONs (Superparamagnetic Iron Oxide Nanoparticles):** MRI contrast + magnetic hyperthermia.
- **mAb–PLGA NPs:** Sustained release of chemotherapeutics.

**Conjugation Methods (1 Mark):**
- **EDC/NHS chemistry** (carbodiimide coupling of carboxyl to amine)
- **Maleimide–thiol coupling** (site-specific on cysteine residues — preserves binding site)
- **Click chemistry (CuAAC)** (azide-alkyne cycloaddition — most specific)
- **Streptavidin–Biotin** (highest non-covalent affinity, K$_D$ ~ 10⁻¹⁵ M)

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):**
mAb-NP conjugates represent the **convergence of molecular biology and nanotechnology**, enabling **active targeting**, **reduced off-target toxicity**, and **theranostic** (therapy + diagnosis) applications — central to modern precision medicine.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a) Explain the principle and procedure of hybridoma technology for the production of monoclonal antibodies. Why is HAT medium used? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 Marks):**
The principle of hybridoma technology is based on the fusion of two cell types:
- **B-lymphocytes** from the spleen of an immunized mouse → produce a specific antibody but cannot proliferate indefinitely (die in culture within 10–14 days).
- **Myeloma cells** (e.g., SP2/0, NS0) → are **immortal** (proliferate forever) but have been engineered to be **HGPRT-deficient**, so they cannot use the salvage pathway.

Upon **PEG-induced fusion**, the resulting **hybridoma** inherits the immortality of the myeloma and the antibody-producing machinery of the B-cell. Each hybridoma is a clonal factory producing a single, identical antibody — the **monoclonal antibody**.

**Procedure (4 Marks — step-wise):**

1. **Immunization of mouse** with antigen + adjuvant (3–4 weeks).
2. **Fusion:** Splenocytes + myeloma cells in 1:5 ratio + 50% PEG for 60–90 s.
3. **HAT Selection** for 10–14 days.
4. **Screening** of hybridoma supernatants by ELISA.
5. **Single-cell cloning** by limiting dilution.
6. **Expansion & purification** via Protein A chromatography.

**HAT Medium — Why? (1 Mark):**
HAT medium contains **H**ypoxanthine, **A**minopterin, and **T**hymidine. Aminopterin blocks the *de novo* DNA synthesis pathway. To survive, cells must use the **salvage pathway** (hypoxanthine → IMP via HGPRT). Since myeloma cells are HGPRT⁻ and B-cells die naturally, **only hybridomas (HGPRT⁺ + immortal) survive**.

---

**(b) With a neat schematic, describe the structure of an IgG monoclonal antibody. Explain the function of its Fab and Fc regions. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Structure (3 Marks):**
An IgG monoclonal antibody is a **Y-shaped glycoprotein** of molecular weight **150 kDa**, consisting of:
- **2 Heavy Chains (H, ~50 kDa each)** — composed of VH, CH1, CH2, CH3 domains.
- **2 Light Chains (L, ~25 kDa each)** — either κ (kappa) or λ (lambda); composed of VL and CL domains.
- **2 Fab regions (Fragment antigen-binding):** the two arms of the "Y," each containing VH, CH1, VL, CL. The variable domains (VH, VL) contain the **CDRs (Complementarity-Determining Regions)** that form the **paratope** — the antigen-binding site.
- **1 Fc region (Fragment crystallizable):** the stem of the "Y," composed of CH2 and CH3 domains from both heavy chains.
- **Hinge region:** flexible linker between Fab and Fc, allowing independent arm movement.
- Chains are linked by **inter-chain disulfide bonds** and stabilized by non-covalent interactions.

**Schematic (Description, 1 Mark):**
A Y-shaped molecule: two upper arms (Fab) bind antigen, lower stem (Fc) interacts with immune cells.

**Functions (3 Marks — 1.5 each):**

**Fab Region Function:**
- **Antigen recognition and binding** with high specificity.
- The **CDRs** (3 in VH + 3 in VL = 6 CDRs total per Fab) form the **paratope** that fits the antigen's **epitope** like a lock-and-key.
- Each mAb has **two identical Fab arms** → **bivalent** → enables **cross-linking** of antigens on cell surfaces (avidity enhancement).

**Fc Region Function:**
- **Effector function recruitment:**
  - Binds **Fcγ receptors (FcγR)** on macrophages, NK cells, dendritic cells → triggers **ADCC (Antibody-Dependent Cell-mediated Cytotoxicity)**.
  - Activates the **complement system** (C1q binding) → **CDC (Complement-Dependent Cytotoxicity)**.
  - Determines **antibody isotype** (IgG1, IgG2, etc.) and **half-life** via **FcRn (neonatal Fc receptor) recycling** in endothelium.
- In therapeutics, the Fc region can be **engineered (e.g., glyco-engineering)** to enhance ADCC or to silence effector function (e.g., in antibody-drug conjugates where only targeting is desired).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the HAT logic:** Many students write "HAT medium kills myeloma cells" — *wrong*. It **selects hybridomas** by killing **both unfused populations**. Always state the **dual selection mechanism**.
> 2. **Confusing the naming convention:** Mixing up "-ximab" (chimeric) and "-zumab" (humanized) is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss. Memorize the table in Section 2.3.
> 3. **Skipping the Fc function:** In Part B questions on antibody structure, many students describe the Fab beautifully but **omit the Fc effector function** (ADCC, CDC, FcRn recycling) — this costs 3+ marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning PEG as the fusogen:** Examiners specifically look for "PEG-induced fusion" — never say "chemicals" vaguely.
> 5. **Forgetting the Nobel Prize context:** Köhler and Milstein (1975) and the 1984 Nobel Prize is a high-yield KTU fact — mention it for extra marks.
> 6. **No mention of ethical restrictions on ascites:** If discussing *in vivo* production, always note that the **ascites method is restricted (3Rs principle — Replacement, Reduction, Refinement)** and *in vitro* bioreactor culture is preferred.
> 7. **mAb-NP question traps:** If asked about mAb-nanoparticle conjugates, students often forget to mention the **EPR (passive targeting) vs. active targeting (mAb)** distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Monoclonal Antibodies**

- [x] **Definition:** Monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) = homogeneous antibodies from a **single B-cell clone**, binding **one specific epitope**.
- [x] **Origin:** Köhler & Milstein, **1975**; **Nobel Prize 1984** (shared with Jerne).
- [x] **Hybridoma = B-cell + Myeloma** (fused via PEG).
- [x] **HAT Selection** = Hypoxanthine + Aminopterin + Thymidine.
- [x] **Aminopterin** blocks *de novo*; cells use **salvage pathway** (requires **HGPRT**).
- [x] **Myeloma is HGPRT⁻**; **B-cell is finite**; **Hybridoma = HGPRT⁺ + Immortal = SURVIVES**.
- [x] **Screening:** ELISA / Western blot / flow cytometry.
- [x] **Cloning:** Limiting dilution ensures monoclonality.
- [x] **Purification:** Protein A/G affinity chromatography.
- [x] **Production:** In vitro (bioreactor) preferred; in vivo (ascites) ethically restricted.
- [x] **Types & Naming:** Murine (-omab), Chimeric (-ximab, e.g., Rituximab), Humanized (-zumab, e.g., Trastuzumab), Fully Human (-umab, e.g., Adalimumab).
- [x] **Structure:** Y-shaped IgG, **150 kDa**, 2 Heavy + 2 Light chains, **2 Fab + 1 Fc**.
- [x] **Fab** = Antigen binding (VH, VL, CDRs).
- [x] **Fc** = Effector function (ADCC, CDC, FcRn recycling).
- [x] **Avidity ≈ (K$_a$)²** for bivalent IgG.
- [x] **mAb-NP Conjugates:** AuNP, Liposome, QD, SPION, PLGA.
- [x] **Conjugation:** EDC/NHS, Maleimide-thiol, Click chemistry, Streptavidin-biotin.
- [x] **Passive targeting:** EPR effect (NPs accumulate in tumors due to leaky vasculature).
- [x] **Active targeting:** mAb binds overexpressed receptor (HER2, EGFR, CD20).
- [x] **Theranostics:** Combined therapy + diagnosis in one construct.
- [x] **Examples:** Trastuzumab (HER2⁺ breast cancer), Rituximab (CD20⁺ lymphoma), Adalimumab (anti-TNF-α), Casirivimab (anti-SARS-CoV-2).
- [x] **NP Size:** 10–200 nm optimal for EPR-based tumor accumulation.
- [x] **Titer formula:** $\text{Titer} = \log_{10}(1/\text{dilution})$.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Monoclonal Antibodies**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme Bionanotechnology (PEBRT743) syllabus and Board Examiner valuation patterns.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microscopy and Imaging Techniques Such as SEM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT743 - INTRODUCTION TO BIONANOTECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Microscopy and Imaging Techniques Such as SEM # 1. Microscopy and Imaging Techniques — Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Microscopy** is the science and technology of producing **magnified images** of objects or specimens that are too small to be resolved by the unaided human eye, using **electromagnetic radiation**, **electron beams**, **ion beams**, or **scanning probes**. In the context of **Bionanotechnology**, microscopy and imaging techniques form the **primary characterization toolkit** for visualizing biological structures (cells, proteins, viruses, DNA) and synthetic nanomaterials (nanoparticles, nanotubes, quantum dots) at length scales spanning from **~100 nm down to ~0.1 nm (atomic scale)**. The two broad classifications are: | Class | Mechanism | Examples | Typical Resolution | |---|---|---|---| | **Optical / Photon-based** | Visible light interaction with specimen | Compound, Fluorescence, Confocal | ~200 nm (Abbe limit) | | **Electron / Particle-based** | Electron-matter interaction | **SEM**, TEM, STEM | ~0.1 – 10 nm | | **Scanning Probe** | Physical tip-sample interaction | AFM, STM | ~0.01 – 1 nm | --- ## 1.2 Focus: Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) > [!NOTE] > **Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)** is an electron microscopy technique that produces high-resolution images of a specimen's surface by **scanning it with a focused beam of electrons** in a **raster scan pattern** and detecting the **secondary electrons (SE)** and **backscattered electrons (BSE)** emitted from the sample surface. The result is a **topographical, 2D projection image** with a depth of field hundreds of times greater than that of an optical microscope, and a **resolving power** roughly **1,000× greater** than light microscopy. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blind Person Reading Braille" > [!TIP] > Imagine you are a **blind person (electron detector)** trying to "feel" the shape of a **carved wooden sculpture (specimen surface)**. Instead of touching the whole object at once, you use your **fingertip (focused electron beam)** and **sweep it line-by-line across the surface (raster scan)**. As you move, your brain **records the bumps, dips, and edges** and **reassembles a complete mental picture**. The closer your fingertip comes to the surface, the finer the details you can sense. > > **This is exactly how SEM works**: the focused electron beam is the "fingertip," the specimen is the "sculpture," and the detector + computer system rebuilds the image pixel by pixel. --- ## 1.4 Why SEM is Critical in Bionanotechnology - **Resolution limit of optical microscopy (~200 nm)** is governed by the **Abbe diffraction limit** (discussed in Section 2). Most biomolecules and nanostructures fall **below this threshold**. - SEM bridges the gap between light microscopy (~200 nm) and TEM (atomic scale), typically achieving **1–10 nm resolution** for surface imaging. - Standard physical constants used throughout this module: > **Key Standard Constants** > - Electron rest mass: $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ **kg** > - Elementary charge: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C** > - Speed of light: $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s** > - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s** > - Vacuum permittivity: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Physics of the SEM ## 2.1 The Abbe Diffraction Limit — Why Optical Microscopy Fails The **theoretical resolution limit** of any optical microscope was derived by **Ernst Abbe (1873)**: $$d = \frac{\lambda}{2 \, n \sin \alpha} = \frac{\lambda}{2 \, \text{NA}}$$ Where: - $d$ = **smallest resolvable distance** (m) - $\lambda$ = **wavelength of illumination** (m) - $n$ = **refractive index** of the medium between lens and specimen - $\alpha$ = **half-angle** of the maximum cone of light entering the lens - $\text{NA} = n \sin \alpha$ = **numerical aperture** > [!IMPORTANT] > For visible light ($\lambda \approx 400$–$700$ nm) and best-case NA $\approx 1.4$, $d \approx 200$ nm. **Any two features closer than ~200 nm cannot be distinguished** by conventional light microscopy — this is the fundamental reason electron microscopy (SEM/TEM) is essential for bionanotechnology. --- ## 2.2 Operational Principles of the SEM — Step-by-Step The SEM operates on **four fundamental stages**: ### Step 1: Electron Generation (Electron Source / Gun) A **thermoionic or field-emission source** emits electrons. Two principal types: - **Thermionic emission (W or LaB₆ filament)**: Filament heated to ~2,700 K; electrons boil off. - **Field emission gun (FEG)**: Extremely sharp tip (~100 nm radius) under a strong electric field; emits electrons by **quantum tunneling** at room temperature (cold FEG) or heated (Schottky FEG). The emitted electrons are then accelerated by a voltage $V$ (typically **0.5 – 30 kV**), gaining kinetic energy: $$E_k = eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$ Solving for electron velocity: $$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The **de Broglie wavelength** of an accelerated electron is: $$\lambda_{e} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}} \quad \text{(non-relativistic)}$$ > For $V = 30$ kV, $\lambda_e \approx 6.98 \times 10^{-12}$ m = **6.98 pm** — about **100,000× shorter** than visible light. This is what gives SEM/TEM their extraordinary resolution. ### Step 2: Beam Focusing (Electromagnetic Lenses) A series of **electromagnetic lenses** (condenser lens, objective lens) and **apertures** focus the electron beam to a **probe diameter of 1–10 nm** at the specimen surface. ### Step 3: Raster Scanning Deflection coils (scan coils) sweep the focused beam across the specimen in a **raster (TV-like) pattern**: point-by-point, line-by-line. ### Step 4: Signal Detection & Image Formation When the primary electron beam strikes the sample, several **interaction products** are generated (see Section 3). The most commonly detected for **SEM imaging** are: | Signal | Origin | Energy | Information Conveyed | |---|---|---|---| | **Secondary Electrons (SE)** | Inelastic scattering from surface atoms | < 50 eV | **Topography** (high surface detail) | | **Backscattered Electrons (BSE)** | Elastic scattering from nucleus | > 50 eV | **Composition / Z-contrast** | | **Characteristic X-rays** | Inner-shell electron transitions | 0.1 – 20 keV | **Elemental analysis (EDS/EDX)** | > [!TIP] > The **detected signal intensity at each scan point** is mapped to a **pixel brightness** on a CRT/LCD display. Synchronizing the scan generator with the display produces the SEM image. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master These Equations — They Appear in KTU University Exams Regularly** | # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $d = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, n \sin \alpha}$ | Abbe diffraction limit | Optical resolution ceiling | | 2 | $E_k = eV$ | Kinetic energy of accelerated electron | Beam energy calculation | | 3 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV}{m_e}}$ | Electron velocity from accelerating voltage | Beam velocity | | 4 | $\lambda_e = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$ | de Broglie wavelength (non-relativistic) | Resolving power | | 5 | $\lambda_{rel} = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e eV \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$ | Relativistic de Broglie wavelength | High-voltage SEM/TEM (≥ 100 kV) | | 6 | $M = \dfrac{L_{screen}}{L_{specimen}}$ | Magnification | Image scaling | | 7 | $D_{probe} = \dfrac{d_{S}}{2 \alpha_{obj}}$ | Probe diameter (Gaussian beam) | SEM resolution | | 8 | $\delta = \dfrac{N_{SE}}{N_{PE}}$ | Secondary electron yield | Signal-to-noise analysis | > **Notation Note:** $N_{SE}$ = number of secondary electrons emitted, $N_{PE}$ = number of primary incident electrons, $\delta$ = secondary yield coefficient (typically 0.1–1.0 for most biological/biological-coated samples at 5–15 kV). --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Bio Utility - **Cell biology**: SEM of freeze-fractured or critical-point-dried cells reveals **membrane ultrastructure**. - **Virology**: Surface morphology of viruses (e.g., SARS-CoV-2, bacteriophages) — historically the only way to "see" them before cryo-EM. - **Nanomedicine**: Drug delivery nanoparticles (liposomes, polymeric NPs) are routinely imaged by SEM for **size, shape, aggregation state** validation. - **Tissue engineering**: SEM of scaffolds (collagen, PLA, hydrogel) for **porosity and fiber diameter** analysis. - **Failure analysis**: Foreign particulates in medical devices, contamination on implants. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relating Accelerating Voltage to de Broglie Wavelength This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module 3. > **Starting Point:** An electron is accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$. **Step 1 — Work-Energy Theorem:** The work done by the electric field on the electron equals its kinetic energy: $$W = q \cdot V = eV = E_k$$ **Step 2 — Kinetic Energy in Terms of Velocity:** $$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$ **Step 3 — de Broglie Hypothesis:** $$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{m_e v}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $v$ from Step 1:** $$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 eV}{m_e}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute into de Broglie Equation:** $$\lambda_e = \frac{h}{m_e \sqrt{\dfrac{2 eV}{m_e}}}$$ **Step 6 — Simplify Algebraically:** $$\lambda_e = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$ This is the **non-relativistic de Broglie wavelength of an accelerated electron** — Equation (4) in the formula sheet. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Validity Check:** This equation holds when $eV \ll m_e c^2 = 511$ keV. For SEM (typically ≤ 30 kV), the assumption is excellent. For TEM (100–300 kV), the relativistic correction (Eq. 5) must be used. ### Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style Problem) > **Q:** Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through 20 kV. Is the non-relativistic approximation valid? **Step 1 — Convert voltage:** $$V = 20 \, \text{kV} = 20{,}000 \, \text{V}$$ **Step 2 — Plug in constants:** $$\lambda_e = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 20{,}000}}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate denominator:** $$2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \times 10^{4}$$ $$= 2 \times 9.109 \times 1.602 \times 2 \times 10^{-31-19+4}$$ $$= 58.37 \times 10^{-46}$$ $$= 5.837 \times 10^{-45}$$ **Step 4 — Square root:** $$\sqrt{5.837 \times 10^{-45}} = 7.640 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{kg·m/s}$$ **Step 5 — Final result:** $$\lambda_e = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{7.640 \times 10^{-23}} = 8.67 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{m}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_e \approx 8.67 \, \text{pm} = 0.00867 \, \text{nm}}$$ **Step 6 — Validity check:** $$eV = 20{,}000 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 3.20 \times 10^{-15} \, \text{J}$$ $$\frac{eV}{m_e c^2} = \frac{3.20 \times 10^{-15}}{8.187 \times 10^{-14}} = 0.039 \ll 1$$ ✅ **Non-relativistic approximation is valid** (< 5% correction). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relativistic de Broglie Wavelength (High-Voltage EM) At $V \geq 100$ kV (typical TEM), we must use relativistic energy-momentum. **Step 1 — Relativistic total energy:** $$E_{total} = m_e c^2 + eV$$ **Step 2 — Relativistic momentum (energy-momentum relation):** $$E_{total}^2 = (p c)^2 + (m_e c^2)^2$$ $$p = \frac{\sqrt{E_{total}^2 - (m_e c^2)^2}}{c} = \frac{\sqrt{(m_e c^2 + eV)^2 - (m_e c^2)^2}}{c}$$ **Step 3 — Expand the difference of squares:** $$(m_e c^2 + eV)^2 - (m_e c^2)^2 = 2 m_e c^2 eV + (eV)^2$$ **Step 4 — Therefore:** $$p = \frac{\sqrt{2 m_e c^2 eV + (eV)^2}}{c} = \sqrt{2 m_e eV + \frac{(eV)^2}{c^2}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute into $\lambda = h/p$:** $$\lambda_{rel} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e eV + \dfrac{(eV)^2}{c^2}}}$$ **Step 6 — Factor out $2 m_e eV$ to match standard textbook form:** $$\lambda_{rel} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e eV \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$$ This recovers the non-relativistic equation when $eV \ll m_e c^2$. ✅ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Imaging Techniques > [!NOTE] > KTU frequently asks **comparison/matrix questions** worth 7–10 marks. Memorize this table. | Parameter | Optical Microscope | SEM | TEM | |---|---|---|---| | **Probe** | Photon (visible light) | Focused electron beam | Transmitted electron beam | | **Wavelength** | 400 – 700 nm | ~1 – 10 pm (relativistic) | ~1 – 5 pm | | **Resolution** | ~200 nm | ~1 – 10 nm | ~0.1 – 0.5 nm | | **Magnification** | ×40 – ×1000 | ×10 – ×1,000,000 | ×50 – ×50,000,000 | | **Image type** | Surface (color) | Surface (B&W, pseudo-color) | Internal / projection | | **Sample state** | Living / fixed | Dry, conductive (or sputter-coated) | Ultra-thin (< 100 nm) | | **Vacuum required** | No | High vacuum | High vacuum | | **Depth of field** | Shallow | Very deep | Moderate | | **Typical voltage** | — | 0.5 – 30 kV | 60 – 300 kV | | **Signal detected** | Transmitted / reflected photons | SE, BSE, X-rays | Transmitted electrons | | **Cost (approx.)** | Low | Moderate – High | Very high | --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3: SEM Resolution from Probe Diameter The ultimate **SEM resolution** is limited by the **size of the electron probe** at the specimen: **Step 1 — Gaussian beam optics:** For a beam of initial crossover diameter $d_S$ (from the gun), demagnified by the objective lens of semi-angle $\alpha_{obj}$: $$D_{probe} = \frac{d_S}{2 \alpha_{obj}}$$ **Step 2 — Sample interaction volume:** In practice, resolution is also limited by the **electron interaction volume** in the sample: $$V_{int} \propto \frac{Z}{E^2} \cdot \rho$$ Where $Z$ = atomic number, $E$ = beam energy, $\rho$ = density. > [!TIP] > **Lower accelerating voltage** (e.g., 1–5 kV) → smaller interaction volume → **better surface resolution** and less beam damage to delicate biological samples. This is why **low-voltage SEM (LVSEM / FE-SEM)** is preferred for biological and polymeric specimens. --- ## 3.5 Sample Preparation — A Critical Practical Step > [!WARNING] > **A common KTU essay-style question:** "Discuss the sample preparation steps for biological SEM imaging." Standard steps for **biological samples** (cells, tissues, biomaterials): 1. **Fixation** — Glutaraldehyde or paraformaldehyde stabilizes structures by cross-linking proteins. 2. **Dehydration** — Graded ethanol series (30% → 50% → 70% → 95% → 100%) removes water. 3. **Drying** — **Critical point drying (CPD)** using liquid CO₂ avoids surface tension collapse. 4. **Mounting** — Specimen fixed onto an aluminum SEM stub with conductive carbon tape. 5. **Sputter coating** — A thin (5–20 nm) layer of gold (Au), platinum (Pt), or gold-palladium is sputtered onto the sample to make it electrically conductive. 6. **Imaging** — Sample loaded into SEM chamber under high vacuum. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — SEM Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Electron Source
W filament or FEG] --> B[Accelerating Anode
0.5 to 30 kV] B --> C[Condenser Lens 1
Beam demagnification] C --> D[Condenser Aperture
Beam current control] D --> E[Condenser Lens 2
Further demagnification] E --> F[Scan Coils X and Y
Raster pattern generation] F --> G[Objective Lens
Final probe focusing 1 to 10 nm] G --> H[Specimen Stage
High vacuum chamber] H --> I1[Secondary Electron Detector] H --> I2[Backscattered Electron Detector] H --> I3[Energy Dispersive X ray Detector] I1 --> J[Signal Amplifier and Processor] I2 --> J I3 --> J J --> K[Display Monitor
SEM Image] F -.sync.-> K classDef source fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000 classDef lens fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#000 classDef sample fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,color:#000 classDef detect fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 classDef display fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000 class A source class B,C,D,E,F,G lens class H sample class I1,I2,I3,J detect class K display ``` --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Electron–Specimen Interaction (Interaction Volume) ```mermaid flowchart TB PE[Primary Electron Beam
Energy 1 to 30 keV] --> SP[Specimen Surface] SP --> SE[Secondary Electrons
Energy less than 50 eV] SP --> BSE[Backscattered Electrons
Energy greater than 50 eV] SP --> TX[Transmitted Electrons
Used in TEM] SP --> XR[Characteristic X rays
Used in EDS EDX] SP --> CL[Cathodoluminescence
Used in mineralogy] SP --> AU[Auger Electrons
Surface analysis] SE --> IMG1[Topographic Image] BSE --> IMG2[Compositional Z contrast Image] XR --> IMG3[Elemental Map] CL --> IMG4[Optical emission map] AU --> IMG5[Surface chemistry] classDef input fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 classDef interact fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c,color:#000 classDef output fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857,color:#000 class PE input class SP,SE,BSE,TX,XR,CL,AU interact class IMG1,IMG2,IMG3,IMG4,IMG5 output ``` --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — SEM vs TEM Imaging Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SEM ES_S[Electron Source] --> BL_S[Electron Lenses] BL_S --> SC_S[Scanning Coils] SC_S --> SS[Specimen Surface] SS --> SE_D[SE or BSE Detector] SE_D --> DISP_S[Surface Image] end subgraph TEM ES_T[Electron Source] --> BL_T[Electron Lenses] BL_T --> ST[Ultra thin Specimen] ST --> TE_D[Transmitted Electron Detector] TE_D --> DISP_T[Internal Projection Image] end classDef semnode fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000 classDef temnode fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000 classDef semall fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000 classDef temall fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000 class ES_S,BL_S,SC_S,SS,SE_D,DISP_S semnode class ES_T,BL_T,ST,TE_D,DISP_T temnode ``` > [!TIP] > **Key visual takeaway from this block architecture:** SEM detects electrons emerging from the **top of the specimen** (surface imaging), whereas TEM detects electrons **passing through** the specimen (internal/transmission imaging). The geometry completely changes the information you obtain. --- ## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Which Microscope to Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD A[What do you want to image?] --> Q1{Surface or Internal} A --> Q2{Living or Fixed} A --> Q3{Resolution needed} Q1 -- Surface --> SEM[Choose SEM] Q1 -- Internal or Atomic --> TEM[Choose TEM] Q2 -- Living --> FL[Choose Fluorescence or Confocal] Q2 -- Fixed --> EM[Choose SEM or TEM] Q3 --> R1{Sub 10 nm} R1 -- Yes --> EM R1 -- No --> OPT[Optical Microscope is sufficient] SEM --> SAMPLE1{Conductive} SAMPLE1 -- Yes --> SEM_OK[Standard SEM Imaging] SAMPLE1 -- No --> SPUT[Sputter coat with Au Pt] TEM --> SAMPLE2{Ultra thin less than 100 nm} SAMPLE2 -- Yes --> TEM_OK[Standard TEM Imaging] SAMPLE2 -- No --> ULTRAM[Use Ultramicrotome to section] classDef choice fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,color:#000 classDef result fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857,color:#000 classDef prep fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#000 class A,Q1,Q2,Q3,R1,SAMPLE1,SAMPLE2 choice class SEM,TEM,FL,EM,OPT,SEM_OK,TEM_OK result class SPUT,ULTRAM prep ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **What is the Abbe diffraction limit? Why does it make electron microscopy necessary for bionanotechnology?** **Model Answer:** The Abbe diffraction limit, derived by Ernst Abbe in 1873, defines the **smallest resolvable distance** between two points in an optical microscope: $$d = \frac{\lambda}{2 \, n \sin \alpha} = \frac{\lambda}{2 \, \text{NA}}$$ For visible light ($\lambda \approx 400$–$700$ nm) and a typical numerical aperture of $\sim 1.4$, the limit is $\mathbf{d \approx 200 \text{ nm}}$. Since most biological macromolecules (proteins, DNA, viruses) and engineered nanomaterials (nanoparticles, nanotubes) have dimensions **well below 200 nm**, conventional optical microscopy cannot resolve them. Electron beams have **de Broglie wavelengths of 1–10 pm** (~100,000× shorter than visible light), making electron microscopy (SEM/TEM) essential for sub-nanometer imaging in bionanotechnology. **(3 marks)** --- > **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Distinguish between secondary electrons (SE) and backscattered electrons (BSE) in SEM.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Secondary Electrons (SE) | Backscattered Electrons (BSE) | |---|---|---| | **Energy** | < 50 eV (low) | > 50 eV (high, up to primary beam energy) | | **Origin** | Inelastic scattering from surface atoms | Elastic scattering from atomic nuclei | | **Escape depth** | 1 – 10 nm (very surface) | 10 – 100 nm (deeper) | | **Information** | Topography (morphology) | Composition / Z-contrast | | **Yield dependence** | Strongly dependent on surface tilt | Strongly dependent on atomic number Z | SE images give **high-resolution topographical contrast**, while BSE images give **compositional contrast** (heavier elements appear brighter). **(3 marks)** --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > ### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle** of a Scanning Electron Microscope. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Derive an expression for the **de Broglie wavelength** of an electron accelerated through a potential difference $V$. Calculate its value for $V = 25$ kV. **(7 marks)** ### ✅ Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Construction and Working of SEM (7 marks) **Construction (Major Components):** 1. **Electron Source / Gun (1 mark)** — Tungsten (W) filament, LaB₆ crystal, or Field Emission Gun (FEG) emits electrons via thermionic or field emission. 2. **Accelerating Anode (0.5 mark)** — Provides accelerating voltage (0.5 – 30 kV) that propels electrons toward the specimen. 3. **Electromagnetic Lenses (1.5 marks)** — Two condenser lenses and one objective lens demagnify the electron beam to a final probe size of 1–10 nm. 4. **Apertures (0.5 mark)** — Apertures limit the beam current and reduce aberrations. 5. **Scan Coils (0.5 mark)** — Deflect the beam in a **raster (X–Y) pattern** across the specimen. 6. **Specimen Stage (0.5 mark)** — Located in a high-vacuum chamber ($10^{-4}$ – $10^{-6}$ Pa) to prevent electron scattering by air molecules. 7. **Detectors (1.5 marks)** — Everhart-Thornley detector (SE), solid-state BSE detector, and Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDS/EDX) detector for elemental analysis. 8. **Display & Computer System (0.5 mark)** — Reconstructs image by synchronizing scan position with detected signal intensity. **Working Principle (1 mark):** The accelerated, focused electron beam strikes the specimen surface point-by-point. **Secondary electrons (SE)** and **backscattered electrons (BSE)** emitted from each point are collected by detectors. The signal intensity at each scan point is mapped to pixel brightness on a display, generating a **magnified topographical image** of the specimen surface with resolution down to 1–10 nm. --- #### Part (b) — Derivation of de Broglie Wavelength (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark):** An electron of charge $e$ accelerated from rest through a potential $V$ gains kinetic energy: $$E_k = eV$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** From classical kinetic energy: $$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2 \implies v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** By de Broglie's hypothesis, the wavelength is: $$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{m_e v}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Substituting $v$: $$\lambda_e = \frac{h}{m_e \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV}{m_e}}} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$ **Step 5 (3 marks):** Numerical calculation for $V = 25$ kV: $$V = 25{,}000 \, \text{V}$$ $$\lambda_e = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 25{,}000}}$$ Denominator: $$2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 2.5 \times 10^{4}$$ $$= 72.96 \times 10^{-46} = 7.296 \times 10^{-45}$$ $$\sqrt{7.296 \times 10^{-45}} = 8.542 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{kg·m/s}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_e = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{8.542 \times 10^{-23}} = 7.76 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{m} \approx 7.76 \, \text{pm}}$$ --- > ### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** > **(a)** Explain the **principle, working, and applications** of SEM in bionanotechnology with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Compare and contrast **SEM and TEM** with respect to principle, resolution, sample preparation, and applications. **(7 marks)** ### ✅ Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Principle, Working, and Applications of SEM (7 marks) **Principle (2 marks):** The SEM produces images by **scanning a focused beam of high-energy electrons (0.5 – 30 kV)** across the specimen surface in a raster pattern. When the primary electrons interact with atoms in the sample, they generate several signals (SE, BSE, X-rays). The most commonly used signal for surface imaging is **secondary electrons (SE)**, which are low-energy electrons (< 50 eV) emitted from the topmost 1–10 nm of the sample. The intensity of the SE signal at each point is collected by an **Everhart-Thornley detector** and converted to pixel brightness, producing a **high-resolution 2D image** of the surface topography. **Working (2 marks):** - Electron beam generated at the source → accelerated → focused by electromagnetic lenses to a 1–10 nm probe. - Scan coils raster the probe across the specimen. - Detectors (SE, BSE, EDS) collect emitted signals. - Synchronization of scan position and signal intensity produces the image on a display. - High vacuum ($10^{-4}$ – $10^{-6}$ Pa) is maintained throughout. **Applications in Bionanotechnology (3 marks):** 1. **Cell and Tissue Imaging** — Visualization of membrane morphology, surface organelles of plant/animal cells after fixation and critical-point drying. 2. **Virus Morphology** — SARS-CoV-2, influenza, bacteriophages imaged at nanoscale. 3. **Drug Delivery Nanoparticles** — Size, shape, distribution, and aggregation analysis of liposomes, polymeric NPs, metallic NPs. 4. **Tissue Engineering Scaffolds** — Porosity, fiber diameter, and surface roughness of biomaterial scaffolds. 5. **Biomedical Implants** — Surface characterization of titanium implants, stents for biocompatibility. 6. **Biosensors** — Morphology of nanostructured sensing surfaces (e.g., ZnO nanowires, graphene sheets). --- #### Part (b) — SEM vs TEM Comparison (7 marks) | Parameter | SEM | TEM | |---|---|---| | **Principle** | Scans surface with focused e-beam; detects SE/BSE | Transmits e-beam through ultra-thin specimen | | **Electron source energy** | 0.5 – 30 kV | 60 – 300 kV | | **Resolution** | 1 – 10 nm | 0.1 – 0.5 nm (atomic) | | **Magnification** | Up to ~10⁶× | Up to ~5 × 10⁷× | | **Image type** | 3D-like topographical surface | 2D internal projection | | **Sample thickness** | Bulk (mm scale) acceptable | Must be < 100 nm (ultramicrotome) | | **Sample preparation** | Fix → Dehydrate → CPD → Sputter coat (Au/Pt) | Fix → Dehydrate → Embed → Ultramicrotome → Stain (uranyl acetate) | | **Vacuum** | High vacuum | Ultra-high vacuum | | **Main signals** | SE, BSE, X-rays | Transmitted electrons (bright/dark field) | | **Cost** | Moderate – High | Very High | | **Typical biological applications** | Cell surface, virus morphology, NPs | Internal organelles, ribosomes, crystal structures | | **3D information** | Excellent depth of field → quasi-3D | Limited (requires tomography) | --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks on SEM questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the square root** in the de Broglie wavelength derivation — examiners specifically check $h/\sqrt{2m_e eV}$, not $h/(2m_e eV)$. (Lose 1–2 marks) > > 2. **Units mismatch** in numericals — Always express $\lambda$ in **pm or nm** at the end, not in meters. Show the conversion step explicitly. > > 3. **Confusing SE and BSE** — Many students write "BSE gives topographical contrast." This is **wrong**; **SE gives topography**, BSE gives compositional Z-contrast. > > 4. **Sample preparation steps incomplete** — Forgetting **critical point drying** or **sputter coating** in a 7-mark essay costs at least 1 mark. Examiners expect a complete sequential protocol. > > 5. **Not mentioning vacuum requirement** — SEM requires high vacuum ($10^{-4}$ – $10^{-6}$ Pa). Forgetting this loses marks in construction questions. > > 6. **Abbe limit used incorrectly** — Some students use NA $\approx 1$ instead of the correct $\text{NA} = n \sin \alpha \leq 1.4$ for oil-immersion lenses. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-density revision notes — read this twice before the exam.** - **Microscopy** = producing magnified images of objects below the resolution of the human eye. - **Abbe limit**: $d = \lambda / (2 \, \text{NA})$ → $\sim 200$ nm for visible light → **why we need electron microscopy**. - **SEM** = Scanning Electron Microscope = surface imaging, raster scan, detects **SE & BSE**. - **TEM** = Transmission Electron Microscope = internal imaging, electron transmitted through ultra-thin sample. - **Electron acceleration**: $E_k = eV$. - **Electron velocity (non-rel)**: $v = \sqrt{2eV/m_e}$. - **de Broglie wavelength (non-rel)**: $\lambda_e = h / \sqrt{2 m_e e V}$. - **Relativistic correction needed** when $V \geq 100$ kV (i.e., $eV \geq 0.05 \, m_e c^2$). - **SE** = low energy (< 50 eV), topographical information, escape depth 1–10 nm. - **BSE** = high energy (> 50 eV), compositional (Z-contrast), escape depth 10–100 nm. - **Resolution hierarchy**: Optical (~200 nm) < SEM (~1–10 nm) < TEM (~0.1–0.5 nm) < AFM/STM (~0.01 nm). - **SEM sample prep**: Fix → Dehydrate → CPD → Mount → Sputter coat (Au/Pt, 5–20 nm). - **TEM sample prep**: Fix → Dehydrate → Embed → Ultramicrotome section (< 100 nm) → Heavy metal stain. - **SEM vacuum**: $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-6}$ Pa. - **SEM components**: Electron gun, accelerating anode, condenser & objective lenses, apertures, scan coils, specimen stage, SE/BSE/EDS detectors, display. - **Bionanotech applications**: Cell imaging, virus morphology, drug-delivery NP characterization, scaffold analysis, biosensor surface imaging, implant biocompatibility. - **Lower kV = better surface resolution + less beam damage** → Low-Voltage SEM (LVSEM) is preferred for biological samples. - **Magnification**: $M = L_{screen} / L_{specimen}$ — purely a geometric ratio, **not** limited by the lens (unlike resolution).

topics.title: AFM and STM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT743 - INTRODUCTION TO BIONANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** AFM and STM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) is a high-resolution type of **Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM)** in which a sharp nanoscale tip — typically mounted on the free end of a flexible microcantilever — is raster-scanned across a sample surface to map the topography, mechanical, electrical, or magnetic properties of the surface by measuring the interatomic forces (van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary, frictional, etc.) acting between the tip apex and the sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AFM is a **non-optical** technique. Its lateral resolution is **sub-nanometer (~0.1 nm)** and vertical resolution is **~0.01 nm (10 pm)**. Unlike electron microscopy, AFM can image samples in **ambient, liquid, and vacuum environments** — a critical advantage for biological specimens.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Blind Man's Cane":**
Imagine a blind man walking through a room filled with furniture. He uses a sensitive cane (the AFM tip) that bends whenever it touches an object. By recording how much the cane bends at every position, he can build a "mental map" of the room. The AFM works the same way: a tiny tip feels the bumps and dips of the sample surface, and these tiny bends are converted (using a laser beam reflected off the cantilever onto a photodetector) into a 3D topographic image.

**Historical Milestone:**
- Invented in **1986** by **Gerd Binnig, Calvin Quate, and Christoph Gerber**.
- A direct descendant of the Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM, 1981).
- Key physical constants used in AFM calculations:
  - **Cantilever spring constant: k ≈ 0.01 – 50 N/m**
  - **Force sensitivity: ~10⁻¹² N (piconewton)**
  - **Tip radius of curvature: ~2 – 20 nm**

> [!NOTE]
> AFM is **the most widely used SPM technique in bionanotechnology** because it does not require the sample to be conductive (unlike STM) and it can image living biological structures in near-physiological conditions.

---

## 1.2 Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
